elements.sent-2411-546: Fig. \ref{fig:rag illustrates the integration of LLMs with RAG, highlighting seamless context retrieval and query refinement.}
elements.sent-2411-547: The integration of LLMs has significantly improved Text-to-SQL performance.
elements.sent-2411-548: On the SPIDER benchmark \cite{Yu2019 , which assesses the generalization capabilities of Text-to-SQL models across a wide variety of databases, the performance of leading models has markedly improved, with the execution accuracy increasing from approximately 54\% to 91\%.}
elements.sent-2411-549: The exact match accuracy, which was initially approximately 5\%, was increased to 82\%.
elements.sent-2411-550: In comparison, the BIRD benchmark \cite{Li_BIRD_2023 , which presents even greater complexity, reveals that the current LLM-based Text-to-SQL models achieve execution accuracy of around 73\%, underscoring the challenges posed by this benchmark.}
elements.sent-2411-551: When delegating information production to human experts, there is a challenge of ''hidden actions'' where the actions of experts are not fully observable or assessable, potentially leading to misalignment of interests \cite{Gurbaxani1990 .}
elements.sent-2411-552: Similarly, when delegated to AI, the issue shifts to the transparency and reliability of outputs.
elements.sent-2411-553: While human experts may act in self interest, leading to agency costs through false information or additional incentives, AI systems present the challenge of output opacity.
elements.sent-2411-554: End users cannot evaluate the correctness of SQL queries and the resulting KPIs until after the implementation, risking decisions based on false information with economic consequences.
elements.sent-2411-555: Consequently, robust techniques are essential for validating the generated SQL queries.
elements.sent-2411-556: Techniques for validating automatically generated SQL queries fall within the broader field of explainable AI \cite{DARPA2016, Rudin2019, Minh2022, Langer2021, Gunning2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-557: In recent years, the growing demand for transparency in machine learning has prompted significant advances in the field of explainable AI, leading to an expansion of both the scope and sophistication of explanation techniques.
elements.sent-2411-558: These explanations must detail the structure of the queries, including the tables, fields, and operations (e.g., filtering, grouping, or joining) involved.
elements.sent-2411-559: Such detailed information enables users to critically evaluate the logical coherence of a query and its alignment with their intended objectives, thereby supporting an independent validation process.
elements.sent-2411-560: Effective validation ensures that users can reliably differentiate between the correct and incorrect SQL queries.
elements.sent-2411-561: However, validation is ineffective if incorrect SQL queries are misclassified as correct, or if correct queries are misclassified as incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-562: Validation in Text-to-SQL extends beyond mere explanation and often encompasses techniques for error correction.
elements.sent-2411-563: These techniques reflect the interactive nature of CBA, and aim to support users in identifying and rectifying errors in SQL queries.
elements.sent-2411-564: In this study, the interpretability of the AI outputs and their correction are treated separately, with a focus on validation effectiveness as the primary metric.
elements.sent-2411-565: The current literature identifies three techniques for validating and explaining SQL queries (see \cite{Zhang2023__ for an overview).}
elements.sent-2411-566: Decomposition techniques (see Fig. \ref{fig:decomposition ) break SQL queries into components such as tables, fields, and operations, presenting intermediate results based on sample data to trace each element's contribution to the final output \cite{Arpit2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-567: Visualization techniques use graphical representations to map relationships among query components, thereby enhancing clarity and navigation in complex query structures \cite{Leventidis2020, Miedema2021, Murakawa2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-568: Dialogue techniques employ LLMs to provide natural language explanations, enabling users to interactively explore and refine queries by addressing specific components or underlying data model \cite{Tian2024, Elgohary2020, Kokkalis2012, Gur2018, Xiaxia2021, yao2019model .}
elements.sent-2411-569: In a user study, Ning et al. observed that decomposition, visualization, and dialogue techniques achieved an average effectiveness rate of approximately 56\% in detecting and correcting errors \cite{Zhang2023__ .}
elements.sent-2411-570: In contrast, Tian et al. demonstrated that their technique, which combines elements of decomposition and dialogue techniques, significantly outperformed existing techniques, achieving an average effectiveness in validation and error correction of approximately 85\% \cite{Tian2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-571: These findings underscore the substantial progress in user-based validation and error correction, showcasing their transformative potential to enhance the reliability of AI-generated SQL queries.
elements.sent-2411-572: Tian et al. showed that their technique consistently delivers effective results regardless of the technical competency of end users \cite{Tian2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-573: However, the boundaries of user-based validation in complex business contexts, such as data warehouses with hundreds of interrelated tables and fields, require further research.
elements.sent-2411-574: Adding to the complexity, the explanatory information in Text-to-SQL differs significantly from the explainable AI approaches commonly used to interpret machine learning models.
elements.sent-2411-575: Explanations in machine learning are typically closely tied to business language, such as: "The model predicts that the customer will have poor creditworthiness because the customer's features exhibit these values." In contrast, the explanatory information in Text-to-SQL has a distinctly technical focus, aiming to improve the user's understanding of how the underlying SQL query operates.
elements.sent-2411-576: The expertise and experience of the end users play a critical role in their ability to comprehend the software code and, consequently, evaluate the correctness of the generated information.
elements.sent-2411-577: Furthermore, the complexity of SQL queries, particularly those involving numerous subqueries and joins, combined with the cognitive load they impose on users, affects their ability to comprehend software code (see, for example, \cite{Munoz2020 , which references developers rather than end users).}
elements.sent-2411-578: Additionally, end users typically perform validation only occasionally when generating new SQL queries to meet a new information need.
elements.sent-2411-579: Consequently, they are unable to develop long-term knowledge of SQL or to gain a deeper understanding of the underlying data model.
elements.sent-2411-580: This challenge has also been noted in traditional self-service analytics \cite{Vujosevic2018 , where users face difficulties in building experiential knowledge of the tools and data models employed for individual information generation.}
elements.sent-2411-581: This study introduces theoretical models grounded in rational choice theory, with a focus on expected utility theory \cite{Neumann2007, Tunney2024 , to evaluate the effective use of CBA.}
elements.sent-2411-582: The examination centers on the example of Text-to-SQL.
elements.sent-2411-583: Two primary influencing factors are considered in this study:.
elements.sent-2411-584: It is assumed that the end user possesses knowledge of the levels of these factors, which influence how effectively AI performs in handling insight-generation tasks, and is able to assess their suitability accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-585: In an ideal scenario, both the accuracy and validation effectiveness would be perfect, ensuring the generation of reliable and actionable information.
elements.sent-2411-586: However, these factors are inherently probabilistic and are subject to imperfections.
elements.sent-2411-587: These models assume a risk-neutral perspective, where the end user has a linear utility function.
elements.sent-2411-588: This simplification allows for a focus on the average outcomes (expected values), while ignoring the variability in potential gains and losses.
elements.sent-2411-589: By assuming linearity, the models quantify all the values in monetary units.
elements.sent-2411-590: The analysis begins with the end user's need for information that cannot be provided through standard reporting.
elements.sent-2411-591: The current value of a KPI is critical for informed business decisions.
elements.sent-2411-592: While the end user has access to data marts, the necessary data reside in data warehouses, requiring an SQL query for extraction.
elements.sent-2411-593: This query retrieves data from the data warehouse, prepares it, and ultimately generates the required KPI.
elements.sent-2411-594: A correctly formulated SQL query produces a valid KPI, enabling optimal decisions and yielding a net profit of \(+1\).
elements.sent-2411-595: Conversely, an erroneous query generates a misleading KPI, resulting in a suboptimal decision and a financial loss of \(-1\).
elements.sent-2411-596: The analysis assumes that the data warehouse contains high-quality, undistorted data, making the correctness of the SQL query the sole determinant of the information quality.
elements.sent-2411-597: Despite possessing expertise in business processes, the end user lacks the technical skills required to effectively apply traditional self-service analytics.
elements.sent-2411-598: Currently, no data engineer is available to create the KPI, and such a resource will only be accessible later.
elements.sent-2411-599: The delayed delivery of the KPI results in a reduced (net) profit \(v\) (where \(0<v<1\)).
elements.sent-2411-600: This profit is lower than the achievable maximum of \(+1\), reflecting the adverse effects of the delayed action.
elements.sent-2411-601: In extreme cases, \(v\) may even approach zero, rendering KPI almost valueless.
elements.sent-2411-602: Consequently, \(v\) serves as a measure of the urgency of KPI procurement, with lower values of \(v\) indicating greater urgency.
elements.sent-2411-603: The KPI generation process can be delegated to an AI equipped with a natural language interface powered by LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-604: During this step, the end user is provided with explanations of the generated SQL query, which is designed to ensure transparency and enable the independent validation of its correctness.
elements.sent-2411-605: If the query is identified as erroneous, it is rejected and no action is taken, avoiding both gain and loss.
elements.sent-2411-606: This idealized approach isolates the roles of information generation and validation, enabling a focused analysis of their individual effects on the effectiveness of Text-to-SQL.
elements.sent-2411-607: The static perspective was also chosen, as iterative processes for information generation and validation within CBA are still under development.
elements.sent-2411-608: It is further assumed that the end user incurs no direct costs (e.g., for data storage, processing, or the use of LLM) when utilizing Text-to-SQL.
elements.sent-2411-609: This aligns with the prevailing practice in traditional self-service analytics, in which no fees are charged for individual instances of information generation.
elements.sent-2411-610: Because traditional self-service analytics is not feasible owing to a lack of expertise, the end user evaluates whether the KPI should be generated via delegation to AI (either PS or FS), or whether it is more advantageous to wait for the data engineer to receive the KPI at a later time.
elements.sent-2411-611: When CBA (either PS or FS) offers a higher expected value than the profit gained through the data engineer, it is preferred.
elements.sent-2411-612: Table \ref{tab:symbols provides the mathematical symbols used in the following and their corresponding explanations.}
elements.figure-2411-016: \caption{Mathematical symbols used and their explanations.} \label{tab:symbols} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{3pt} \begin{tabular}{p{60pt}p{360pt}} Symbol Explanation $\alpha$ accuracy $\beta$ validation effectiveness $v$ profit obtainable through data engineer $E_{PS}(\alpha)$ expected value in PS $E_{FS}(\alpha, \beta)$ expected value in FS $\alpha_{PS}^*$ accuracy threshold for PS to outperform data engineer $\alpha_{FS}^*$ accuracy threshold for FS to outperform data engineer $\beta^*$ threshold for validation effectiveness to outperform data engineer $\beta^{**}$ threshold for validation effectiveness to outperform PS \end{tabular} \label{tab1}
elements.sent-2411-613: The success of PS depends primarily on the accuracy of AI in translating natural language requests into correct SQL queries.
elements.sent-2411-614: As outlined earlier, current advancements, such as fine-tuning combined with prompt design, are employed to enhance AI's information generation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-615: The probability of generating a correct SQL query, denoted as \(\alpha\) (where \(0 < \alpha < 1\)), corresponds to execution accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-616: The expected value \(E_{PS (\alpha)\) under PS is calculated as the weighted sum of possible outcomes.}
elements.sent-2411-617: A correct SQL query yields a profit of \(+1\), whereas an incorrect SQL query results in a loss of \(-1\).
elements.sent-2411-618: These outcomes are weighted by the probabilities of success (\(\alpha\)) and failure (\(1-\alpha\)), respectively.
elements.sent-2411-619: Therefore, the expected value is expressed as follows: \begin{equation}E_{PS}(\alpha) = \alpha \cdot (+1) + (1-\alpha) \cdot (-1) = 2\alpha - 1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-620: Delegation to AI through PS is preferred over waiting for a data engineer when the expected value \(E_{PS (\alpha)\) exceeds the delayed profit (\(v\)).}
elements.sent-2411-621: This condition can be formalized as the ''AI delegation condition of PS'': \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} E_{PS}(\alpha) > v \Leftrightarrow \alpha^*_{PS} > (1+v)/2. \end{aligned} \label{eq:cond_1}\end{equation} Condition \eqref{eq:cond_1 defines the threshold of accuracy that must be exceeded for PS to be more advantageous than relying on a data engineer.}
elements.sent-2411-622: When this condition is met (\(\alpha > \alpha^*_{PS}\)), PS is viable and enables effective KPI generation without relying on human expertise.
elements.sent-2411-623: Conversely, if this condition is not satisfied (\(\alpha \leq \alpha^*_{PS}\)), the expected value from PS falls below the profit achievable through delegation to a data engineer, rendering PS unsuitable for time-sensitive, decision-critical business analytics.
elements.sent-2411-624: In the second phase, the AI-generated SQL query is reviewed by the end user to determine whether a correct KPI has been generated, ensuring transparency about the functionality of the SQL query.
elements.sent-2411-625: Techniques, such as decomposition, visualization, and dialogue-based interaction, are currently being explored to support users in comprehending SQL queries.
elements.sent-2411-627: Both the user’s comprehension and the complexity of the SQL query and its underlying data model significantly influence the success of validation.
elements.sent-2411-628: Additionally, inherent uncertainty arises in dialogue-based interactions because AI-generated explanations are based on probabilities rather than deterministic rules.
elements.sent-2411-629: To address this uncertainty, the validation effectiveness is modeled as a random variable denoted as \(\beta\) (where \(0 < \beta < 1\)).
elements.sent-2411-630: The end user takes action only if an SQL query is classified as correct.
elements.sent-2411-631: The probability of correctly classifying a correct SQL query is \(\alpha \cdot \beta\), resulting in a gain of \(+1\).
elements.sent-2411-632: Conversely, the probability of incorrectly classifying an incorrect SQL query as correct is \((1-\alpha) \cdot (1-\beta)\), which results in a loss of \(-1\).
elements.sent-2411-633: However, if a correct SQL query is mistakenly classified as incorrect or an incorrect SQL query is correctly classified as incorrect, no action is taken, and neither a gain nor a loss occurs.
elements.sent-2411-634: Thus, the end user's expected value \(E_{FS (\alpha, \beta)\) when FS is provided is given by \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} E_{FS}(\alpha, \beta) = \alpha \beta \cdot (+1) + (1-\alpha)(1-\beta) \cdot (-1) = \alpha + \beta - 1. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The expected value \(E_{FS (\alpha, \beta)\) is influenced by two key factors: accuracy and validation effectiveness.}}
elements.sent-2411-635: Higher values of \(\alpha\) indicate improved accuracy in generating correct SQL queries, whereas higher values of \(\beta\) represent enhanced effectiveness in validating the correctness of those queries.
elements.sent-2411-636: This relationship highlights the complementary role of accuracy and validation effectiveness in ensuring the success of FS in CBA.
elements.sent-2411-637: The end user will choose FS only if it provides a higher expected value than the profit achievable through delegation, \begin{equation}E_{FS}(\alpha, \beta) > v,\end{equation} and offers a better outcome than PS, \begin{equation}E_{FS}(\alpha, \beta) > E_{PS}(\alpha).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-638: For FS to outperform delegation to a data engineer, the following condition, referred to as the ''AI delegation condition for FS'' must be satisfied: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} E_{FS}(\alpha, \beta) > v \Leftrightarrow \beta^* > (1 - \alpha) + v. \end{aligned} \label{eq:cond_2}\end{equation} Condition \eqref{eq:cond_2 requires the validation to be sufficiently effective and robust (e.g., against the shortcomings of the end user conducting the validation based on the provided explanation) to compensate for inaccuracies in the AI-generated information ((\(1 - \alpha\))) and to deliver an overall expected value exceeding the guaranteed profit \(v\).}
elements.sent-2411-639: However, this preference also depends on the accuracy of AI-generated information being sufficiently high compared to the profit \(v\).
elements.sent-2411-640: Specifically, the ''FS feasibility condition'' must hold: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \alpha^{*}_{FS} > v. \end{aligned} \label{eq:cond_3}\end{equation} Condition \eqref{eq:cond_3 ensures that the expected gain (\(\alpha \cdot (+1)\)) without validation exceeds the guaranteed profit, \(v\).}
elements.sent-2411-641: If this condition is not satisfied, it is not justified to use FS, even with perfect validation effectiveness, as the AI's base accuracy would be insufficient to outperform human delegation.
elements.sent-2411-642: However, whether FS is advantageous compared with delegation to a data engineer depends on the level of validation effectiveness, as defined in the AI delegation condition for FS \eqref{eq:cond_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-643: This requirement is formalized as the ''validation dominance condition'': \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} E_{FS}(\alpha, \beta) > E_{PS}(\beta) \Leftrightarrow \beta^{**} > \alpha. \end{aligned} \label{eq:cond_4}\end{equation} All \(\beta\) values that satisfy \eqref{eq:cond_4 make FS more advantageous than PS.}
elements.sent-2411-644: It is important to note that all the conditions described so far depend on the values of the potential gain (\(+1\)) and loss (\(-1\)).
elements.sent-2411-645: If the value of the loss outweighs that of the gain, the threshold for \(\beta\) in the validation dominance condition shifts, allowing a less stringent validation standard for FS to outperform PS.
elements.figure-2411-017: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{result_final_adal.png} \caption{Relationship between accuracy (\(\alpha\)) and validation effectiveness (\(\beta\)): conditions for effective AI delegation.} \label{fig:result} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-646: Fig. \ref{fig:result illustrates the relationship between accuracy, validation effectiveness and the profit achievable through delegation to the data engineer (all values are measured in monetary units).}
elements.sent-2411-647: The dotted area highlights the range of \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) values where FS outperforms PS and the data engineer, as all three conditions regarding AI delegation \eqref{eq:cond_2 , feasibility of FS \eqref{eq:cond_3 , and dominance of validation \eqref{eq:cond_4 are satisfied.}}}
elements.sent-2411-648: The figure also identifies the threshold \( \alpha^*_{PS}\) which is determined by the AI delegation condition for PS \eqref{eq:cond_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-649: In region \(A\), the FS feasibility condition \eqref{eq:cond_3 is not met, because the accuracy of information generation is less than or equal to the profit achievable through the data engineer (\(\alpha \leq v\)).}
elements.sent-2411-650: In this region, neither PS nor FS offer sufficient expected values.
elements.sent-2411-651: PS fails owing to its low accuracy, and FS is unable to compensate for this limitation, even when validation effectiveness would be perfect.
elements.sent-2411-652: In this region, CBA is not utilized because it fails to match the effectiveness of the data engineer, rendering it unsuccessful.
elements.sent-2411-653: Furthermore, if the data engineer provides the KPI with significant delay, rendering it irrelevant for the end user due to its diminished value for timely decision-making (\(v\) approaching zero), this not only signifies the failure of CBA but also highlights a broader limitation of business analytics in fulfilling its fundamental objective of delivering timely and decision-relevant information.
elements.sent-2411-654: Moreover, the requirements for accuracy in PS and FS, as well as for validation effectiveness in FS, increase as \(v\) rises.
elements.sent-2411-655: A reduction in urgency causes a rightward shift of \(v\), \(\alpha^*_{PS}\), and the delegation threshold condition for FS \eqref{eq:cond_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-656: In other words, the less urgent the KPI (making it less problematic to wait for the data engineer), the greater the performance demands on CBA to generate the correct SQL query and support the reliable assessment of its correctness.
elements.sent-2411-657: In Region \(B\), FS feasibility condition \eqref{eq:cond_3 is met (\(\alpha > v\)), but the AI delegation condition for PS \eqref{eq:cond_1 is not satisfied (\(\alpha \leq \alpha^*_{PS}\)).}}
elements.sent-2411-658: However, FS becomes a viable option if the validation effectiveness satisfies the AI delegation condition for FS \eqref{eq:cond_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-659: This boosting effect enables FS to enhance the overall reliability of the information, compensating for the limitations of lower accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-660: For example, even if the accuracy falls below the threshold required for PS \eqref{eq:cond_1 (e.g., \(\alpha_1\)), a sufficiently high validation effectiveness in FS (e.g., \(\beta_1\)) can still yield an expected value greater than \(v\).}
elements.sent-2411-661: In region \(C\), where the AI delegation condition for PS \eqref{eq:cond_1 is met (\(\alpha > \alpha^*_{PS}\)), PS becomes a viable alternative to delegation to the data engineer as it provides sufficient benefit on its own.}
elements.sent-2411-662: If the AI delegation condition for FS \eqref{eq:cond_2 and the validation dominance condition \eqref{eq:cond_4 are not fulfilled (e.g., at \(\alpha_2\) and \(\beta_2\)), FS has a ''devastating effect'' because the validation process undermines the reliability of the SQL queries generated.}}
elements.sent-2411-663: Therefore, AI-based assistance for KPI generation with AI-supported validation is not recommended.
elements.sent-2411-664: This underscores the inherent risk of relying on user-based validation without robust mechanisms to support the correctly evaluation of AI-generated SQL queries.
elements.sent-2411-665: FS becomes favorable when, in region \(C\), conditions \eqref{eq:cond_2 and \eqref{eq:cond_4 are fulfilled (e.g., at \(\beta_2\) and \(\alpha_3\)).}}
elements.sent-2411-666: As the accuracy increases, the requirements for validation effectiveness must also increase to ensure that FS remains beneficial.
elements.sent-2411-667: A particular challenge arises when the accuracy is very high; in such cases, user-based validation must become even more effective to provide added value over PS.
elements.sent-2411-668: This places significant demands on validation mechanisms, particularly in terms of users' ability to evaluate and assess complex SQL queries based on the given explanations.
elements.sent-2411-669: This study analyzes CBA, an emerging approach with considerable potential to address the skill gaps inherent in traditional self-service analytics.
elements.sent-2411-670: This study focuses on Text-to-SQL, a semantic parsing technology that has seen transformative advancements through the application of LLMs, enhancing its ability to translate natural language requests into structured SQL statements.
elements.sent-2411-671: PS is effective when the accuracy (\(\alpha\)) exceeds a specified threshold, as outlined in condition \eqref{eq:cond_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-672: FS, however, is only beneficial when three conditions are met: AI delegation condition \eqref{eq:cond_2 , FS feasibility condition \eqref{eq:cond_3 , and validation dominance condition \eqref{eq:cond_4 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-673: This study highlights the importance of validation effectiveness (\(\beta\)) in the context of FS.
elements.sent-2411-674: FS can exert a ''boosting effect'' on information generation, particularly when the validation is robust.
elements.sent-2411-675: Even with low accuracy ( \(\alpha\)), a positive benefit can be derived from the delegation process by preventing incorrect information from being incorporated into operational decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-676: However, the reliance on user-based validation introduces significant uncertainties, especially when users lack the necessary expertise or when SQL queries and data models are highly complex.
elements.sent-2411-677: Without such mechanisms, the advantages of CBA -- particularly when the accuracy (\(\alpha\) ) of translating natural language requests into correct information is high -- could be significantly diminished.
elements.sent-2411-678: A promising avenue for future research is the development of hybrid approaches that combine existing techniques (e.g., decomposition, visualization and dialogue) to support code comprehension with automated validation techniques.
elements.sent-2411-679: Future studies could explore the implementation of automated methods for user-independent assessment of the reliability of the generated KPIs.
elements.sent-2411-680: Potential approaches might include statistical methods, such as confidence intervals, to evaluate the correctness of KPIs, and cross-validation with previously validated reports and KPIs.
elements.sent-2411-681: Integrating such methods could mitigate the risk of misclassification owing to a lack of expertise, thereby enhancing the robustness of CBA.
elements.sent-2411-682: First, the gain from making the correct decision is fixed at \(+1\) and the loss from making the wrong decision is set to \(-1\).
elements.sent-2411-683: Although these values determine the derived conditions, they may not fully capture the variability observed in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-684: The models also disregard the costs associated with using LLMs, data storage, and processing, potentially leading to overestimation of the economic feasibility of CBA.
elements.sent-2411-685: Extending these models to incorporate dynamic, cost-sensitive approaches to information generation and account for data quality considerations could provide deeper and more realistic understanding.. \bibliographystyle{plain.}
